title: Applying a bonding coat before a skim finish
subtitle: Build up a keyed base on low-suction backgrounds before skimming
excerpt: How to apply bonding plaster as a preparatory undercoat on painted walls, plasterboard, or previously sealed surfaces where the skim coat would not bond without a mechanical key. Covers mixing, application, and scratching the surface before the skim is applied.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A gypsum-based undercoat plaster (such as Thistle Bonding) applied to low-suction backgrounds to provide a mechanical key for the finish skim coat. Bonding coat contains a fine aggregate that creates a rough surface once set.
The process of scoring the surface of a set bonding or scratch coat with a comb, serrated trowel, or nails driven through a piece of timber, to create a mechanical key for the next plaster coat.

A surface texture that allows one plaster coat to mechanically bond to the surface beneath it. On smooth backgrounds (painted walls, old enamel), there is no key for the skim coat, so a bonding coat is applied first.

content.text: A
bonding coat
is applied when the background does not provide enough
key

for a skim coat to adhere. Common situations include a painted wall, a plasterboard surface that has been sealed with PVA, or masonry that has been treated with a sealer. Without a bonding coat, the skim coat slides or sags during application and detaches once set.
When to use bonding coat
Painted walls or ceilings being replastered without stripping the paint.
Plasterboard that has been sealed or is being repaired in sections.
Old glazed or dense surfaces where the skim coat would have no adhesion.
Lath-and-plaster repairs (see the companion tutorial).
Step 1, prepare the background

Wash down painted surfaces to remove grease. Lightly sand any high-gloss areas to break the sheen. Remove any loose paint with a scraper. If there are any hollows deeper than 5 mm, fill them with the bonding coat first and allow to set before applying the full coat.
Step 2, apply PVA bonding agent

For high-suction or previously sealed backgrounds, apply a coat of PVA bonding agent diluted 1 part PVA to 3 parts water, and allow to become tacky. Apply the bonding plaster into the tacky PVA. On normal painted surfaces with light preparation, the PVA is optional; most professionals use it as standard on difficult backgrounds.
Step 3, mix and apply the bonding coat

Mix bonding plaster powder into clean water, not the other way around. The correct consistency is a smooth, creamy paste that holds a peak without slumping. Apply with the trowel to a thickness of 8 to 11 mm, building the surface up to approximately 3 mm below the intended finished face. The bonding coat does not need to be perfectly smooth; it is the base coat.
Step 4, scratch the surface

When the bonding coat has firmed up but not fully set (typically 1 to 2 hours), scratch the surface with a plasterer's comb or a piece of timber with nails driven through it. The scratching creates the
mechanical key
for the skim coat. Run the comb in horizontal lines at 50 mm intervals. The scratches do not need to be deep: 2 to 3 mm is enough.
Step 5, allow to set before skimming

Allow the bonding coat to set and firm up before applying the skim coat. Full set takes approximately 2 hours; the surface should be firm but not hard-dried. Applying skim over a bonding coat that is still soft causes the skim to drag and leave lines. If skimming is deferred more than a day, wet the bonding coat surface lightly before applying the skim.
What a correct result looks like

The set bonding coat is a grey, uniformly textured surface with visible horizontal scratch marks at 50 mm intervals. It is firm under the knuckle and does not flex or move. The skim coat applied over it bonds well and does not exhibit any pull-away or cracking at the bonding-to-skim interface.
Common mistakes
Scratching too late.
Bonding coat that has fully hardened is too rigid to scratch effectively. The scratches must be cut while the plaster is still firm but workable.
Applying bonding coat more than 11 mm thick in one pass.
Thick applications slump before setting. Build up in two passes with a scratch between each if more than 11 mm of build-up is needed.
